entry: Today's work kept circling back to one problem: coherence — keeping a curriculum, or a whole research program, pointed in the same direction even as it grows in a dozen directions at once. Dustin proposed a way to connect the lab's own competency model to the frameworks European teacher education already uses — DigCompEdu, UNESCO's AI competency framework, Germany's KMK digital-skills framework — so a claim like 'this curriculum builds digital competence' can be checked against, and combined with, what the wider field already means by that phrase, instead of every project quietly inventing its own vocabulary. The curriculum-evolution manual gained new theory about how learners build understanding in layers, which is the same coherence question seen from the learner's side rather than the standards side. The book collaboration with Susan Hanisch moved forward, refining how the app will show where evolution actually shows up — or quietly doesn't — across German biology curricula. And KoMet, a proposed tool for keeping teacher-education curricula coherent across both grade levels and subjects, got real strategic groundwork toward its funding proposal. Alongside all of that, quieter housekeeping happened too — a public dashboard, contributor guides, cleanup across the lab's own repositories — mostly so a research program this wide, spanning theory, tools, a book, and a grant at once, can stay as coherent as the curricula it's trying to help fix.
generated_by: Claude, in conversation with Dustin Eirdosh
